item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If this was a 4000 entry, I'm inclined to say it is far better than Taboo. While "there is no canon", it seems each X-kilo entry produces a significant theme that resonates throughout the lore. I'm not seeing that with the winner this time, certainly not nearly the impact of 3000, 2000, or 1000.

This presents a pretty simple, yet terrifying idea, that some unknowable god (no, not a Sarkic flesh deity, class 5 green wizard, or flying spaghetti monster, but an omnipotence) punishes SCP staff.

Though you might say that 4069 actually secures their souls, contains them in a chamber, and protects them from the pain of the infinite deaths they suffer.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great and funny format (love these unorthodox scips). I'm usually not the biggest fan of the future-based SCP:s but this works!

+1 for me.
Nice format screw! I thought that there was a Wikidot bug/glitch that made a link to a non-existent page look like a link to a page that DOES exist, but then, there were links to the REAL article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is very good.

I thought the format screw was handled excellently, and it had depth without being unnecessarily long. I liked the extensive use of alternate canon. The exploration logs were also very good, and that's rare.

Nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a wonderfully chilling piece. I think the final iteration is a shade weak in essentially being a very verbose expository note, but it still works oddly well regardless. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much to the Chinese Branch for translating this! Their translation is here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those are all reasonable suggestions, and I made about 90 percent of the edits you suggested. There were a few places that I didn't want to change (I agree with your original reasoning about noticing the M37, for instance) but overall that was very helpful. Thanks, and I'll look out for the other section.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm mad as hell, I misclicked and closed the tab that had 3/4 of my post in it.

I'm going to say 'should' and similar a lot, as though I'm some kind of authority, but I'm not one here.

Offset 5

+ show block
– hide block

Broadly, I would leave this offset's editing quality alone; it makes sense that it'd be a littly iffy, since it's being written by a security guard rather than a containment specialist, and in adverse conditions besides.

"As there is no known exit to SCP-4000, it is assumed that no contact between SCP-4000 and the outside world can occur under current circumstances." This is a little glaring, since C-51174 has already contacted the outside world, and is in fact in the process of contacting it even as he types; 'no travel between' or 'no access to' would be sensical observations.

"Although threats are sometimes present in SCP-4000-A, no damage done to the affected person is _not_ permanent." 'Sometimes' isn't usually used in technical writing. We don't have anything more specific to say about the frequency of threats, so we shouldn't say anything more.

"The construction of this room, as well as the materials present in this room, indicate that it is or was at one point attached to a modern Foundation facility." 'The construction and materials of this room are consistent with a modern Foundation facility.' is consistent with modern Foundation phrasing. Since it's not any specific room from a Foundation facility, we shouldn't declare certainty about it; the construction and materials only have the strength to 'suggest' that.

You already ninja'd in 'one cubicle, one computer, one webcam'. Excellent.

"Behind this door is an additional extradimensional area, designated SCP-4000-A, which can contain a variety of environments and hazards"
'Can contain' implies that there are other things which it can contain besides this variety of environments. 'Contains' would be the other extreme, that we are certain that this variety of environments is the only thing in it. 'containing a variety of environments' captures that we know it contains these environments, and doesn't assert that we know anything else.

"with seemingly no correlation between them." This says that the hazards are not correlated to the environments, which doesn't seem to be what is meant. "uncorrelated variety of environments and hazards" better captures that the environment/hazard instances don't correlate with other instances.

"A humanoid entity, SCP-4000-B, will sometimes emerge from SCP-4000-A." 'Will sometimes' is an assertion about a future certainty, which we should avoid making unless we specifically mean it. 'emerges periodically/occasionally/etc' is an observation, which is what we're making.

"whose eyes are obscured by a black bar as if [REDACTED]" please do this no dont

"This individual has only worn the same black and white three-piece suit in all manifestations to date." 'This individual' might be better as SCP-4000-B explicitly. If not, 'the entity', perhaps. If we mean that it is literally, physically the same suit that the thing has been wearing then this phrasing is alright, but if he just likes three-piece suits in general then it's fine to save some characters and say 'has worn a' instead.

Regarding SCP-4000-B's hostility, it did attack C-51174 on one occasion, which is significant, and the exception should be noted.

Offset 5 Tests

+ show block
– hide block

Test #1: If he's a security dude then it seems legit that he's into guns, and an Ithaca 37 is instantly recognizable if you know what one is, but instantly recognizing it as a first-run instance while being chased by a lizard is a bit much.

Test #2: I'm confused by 'exits', but I'm pretty sure this is just me. Saying 'exits' sounds like he leaves the 4000-A instance and goes somewhere else, rather than returning to the cubicle room.

Test #3: 'Excreting' seems like it might be better as 'exuding' or 'secreting' or 'producing'. Excretion is specifically dumping waste with a waste dumping mechanism, while those alternatives are more general. Too, 'seemingly' is probably not necessary. If the fluid is corroding something, it is corrosive, and there's nothing unusual to the Foundation about biomass generating corrosive fluids.

Test #4: I like it.

Test #5: I want to rephrase this significantly. "graphically violent, thematically similar footage of contemporary Foundation operations and incidents from C-51174's personal life." 'thematically similar' needs to be moved inside the descriptor phrase or it wants to make all of the footage thematically similar to some other thing, rather than making the foundation and personal footage similar to each other. Somehow. 'contemporary' goes for '20th and 21st century' because those would only be called out by name if neither of them was current.

Test #12: A small carrier in world war 2 would be an 'escort carrier', although he may not know that. Also, JG and Master Chief look so gawky spelled out, but that's not your problem.

Offset 6 to come, i've run out out battery

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

It remains awkward to me the way Personnel Identification Implants and the ED-K++ encoding language are product-placed; the mode of operation of the implants comes up naturally later in the document, and it feels like it flows more smoothly to bring it up there. Even then, it doesn't quite seem relevant.

PII installation is the only method found that can protect someone from getting stuck in this anomaly after death. It's incredibly relevant, and especially so to the containment procedures section.

Ordinarily I sort of dislike 'pilot episode' entries, but I sort of like 'good' entries, and this is clearly one, or I wouldn't have spent two hours picking nits from it.

Not sure what you mean about this being a pilot in some way — it's set in the same year period and has the same character as some of my other stuff, but it's not the beginning, middle, or end of it, it's just related.

And do you want to put the line-by-line in a collapsible in your post? I'm a pretty rigorous stickler for SPaG and coherent sentence structure, so I'm definitely curious/nervous to see what you found that takes up that much space. :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kinda liked the format screw?

That might be retrospective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wrote up literally two tabloid pages of editor's notes on my way through this, but almost all of it was style/phrasing 'corrections'; probably best shared directly, rather than in a discussion post, or it'd be a screen long.

I thought I had something worth calling out here but I guess it's really all phrasing stuff.

It seemed awkward that the guy noted that he and his buddies were carrying first-run M37s while being pursued by a lizard, but since he was documenting this after the fact he had plenty of time to notice that he noticed.

It remains awkward to me the way Personnel Identification Implants and the ED-K++ encoding language are product-placed; the mode of operation of the implants comes up naturally later in the document, and it feels like it flows more smoothly to bring it up there. Even then, it doesn't quite seem relevant.

Ordinarily I sort of dislike 'pilot episode' entries, but I sort of like 'good' entries, and this is clearly one, or I wouldn't have spent two hours picking nits from it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was just barely good enough to make up for the extremely annoying format screw. Well played.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This really takes its time getting to the big punch. Personally, I found the offsets needed to get to the actual article more an annoyance than anything else, as the "Your SCP is in another castle" trope has been done to death.

The fact that this is a means of viewing the history of the Foundation from the perspective of deceased operatives does leave a lot to think about. I think a tale about the Foundation slamming people through this to find out how a particular employee died would be rather fascinating. Additionally, the ever-changing personification of the SCPF is also a clever bit of characterization.

+1. You spin your wheels pretty heavily there in the beginning but you certainly hit your mark in the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd already started writing the tale series this is linked to re: his future and what happens to him, so I wrapped that part up as best I could in the History section of the final iteration, but I get how it could have been good if his narrative was different and had him in 4000 indefinitely or something so that he could keep serving as a window into it — of course, any of the E/4000 personnel can do that if they're willing and able to communicate through IntSCPFN, which is why I was okay taking him out of there. Regardless, glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of the most impressively-built discoveries I've encountered. The first few iterations were a fantastic build-up (that first page trolled the hell out of me though).

My only (very minor) issue was the final iteration, as I was expecting this to unfold in an entirely different manner by focusing on more on C-51174. I still enjoyed it for what it was, but the shift in focus became a bit disappointing when I reached the end and realized there wasn't going to be more exploration of this purgatory and C-51174.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great - both the concept and execution are brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is amazing! I hope this takes one of the top two slots.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This may actually solve a problem that have with plotting for the next Omega-0 tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The ways this might interact with another way this is true are dizzying.

Goodness, you're right. In reading that one, I realize that these two post-death things could coexist and mesh pretty well together in a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is very clever.

Foundation employment does not end and has never ended after death,

The ways this might interact with another way this is true are dizzying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Foundation employment does not end and has never ended after death, and SCP-4000 is simply another obstacle soon to be rendered obsolete by the advancement of technology.

DON'T FORGET: YOU'RE HERE FOREVER

Man this is clever. Format screws have always been a personal favourite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man we've been getting some cool format screws out of the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was a bit doubtful at first, but the way you tied all the excursions beyond the door together was very tidy and fits the theme of the competition well.

That's awesome to hear, as the cohesion of that one section was the main thing I was still a little worried about despite having reviewers say it was good to go. Thanks and glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I saw that, and it also comes through, but only in the very end. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like that you seem to be using the 'history' theme to mean 'page history'

I have a little blurb in the author post about how it deals with 'real' history, but this is a great additional reason that also works.

Also shall fix typo (thank you)!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
THIS ARTICLE HAS NOT YET BEEN APPROVED FOR PUBLISHING BY INTSCPN AUTHORITIES.

From offset 5. I think you're missing an F there?

I was very confused by the presence of -B, because I thought it referred to the person in the image, but that's Mr. C-numbers. D: Argh!

Not a huge fan of the sci-fi mumbo-jumbo personality restoration device. That said, this is a great format screw, and I like that you seem to be using the 'history' theme to mean 'page history', which I think I a clever subversion. Despite my misgivings and confusion, I think this is actually a pretty darned good entry. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very enjoyable. Looking forward to checking out the cross-linked tales, too. I was a bit doubtful at first, but the way you tied all the excursions beyond the door together was very tidy and fits the theme of the competition well. A much more subtle tribute to foundation history than some of the other entries, while managing to stand on its own legs as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well played! I thought the page was broken at first, then went back when I saw it was getting upvotes.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
acknowledgements
acknowledgements

Big thank you to PeppersGhost for chatting with me about how to make ListPages happen and to Croquembouche for help making that HTML work by being not HTML anymore. Big thank you to Varaxous, stormfallen, AbsentmindedNihilist, WrongJohnSilver, and salvagebar for reading this in draft stages.

on canon
on canon

Stuff like PIIs and ED-K is from my Broken Masquerade stuff and more directly my Doomsday Contest stuff. It's not necessary to read that stuff to understand this — that's what the footnotes are for — though some of that universe is crosslinked in here for good measure.

In terms of the canon this establishes/would potentially establish as 4000, I do not think that all SCPs set after 4000 need to be set in the 2040s, don't worry. The specific number 4000 could have been out of range for a number of reasons, and I emphasize 'there is no canon' when it comes to each SCP's place in the universe.

Ha-ha, Trauss took sixty-nine.

And we're in a 2040s AU where the 4000s are out of range I guess. IDK I'll think up some bullshit to explain it

on history
because a few reviewers said people might not get the theme connection

The SCP-4069 experience is the history of the Foundation, experienced by individual personnel through the eyes of other individual personnel, no matter the time period. Edit: And it's also page history, which I did not even think of, so thank you TL333s for pointing that out downthread.

image info
image info

Images are created by me in full. Released under CC-by-SA-3.0.

spoilers
spoilers

SCP-4069-B is the Foundation. Put more clinically, it's the physical embodiment and representation of how the affected employee sees the Foundation and its influence on their life; hence, its appearance varies greatly and its behavior is neutral-to-hostile, occasionally kind, with neutrality and silent observation being the most common.

SCP-4069-A is its own place. It does its own thing. Sure, most of the time it will take the affected person to a random point in history at or near the death of a different Foundation employee, but some of 51174's logs are original events unique to SCP-4069-A. (Which ones are which is up to you to decide. Whatever forces are behind 4069 wouldn't want you to know the difference.) What does it mean that 4069-A generates Foundation-themed events? Beats me, but given the PII's implications… I'd say it's all in your head.
